answer:
**Slokantara (unknown author), Chapter 1, śloka 47.**

This verse argues that nothing is without a flaw—even natural beauties, celestial bodies, and gods themselves have defects—and therefore humans on earth are even more inevitably flawed.[^1]

> *The lotus is full of thorns; the snowy mountain has its cold; the sandalwood tree is inhabited by serpents;*  
> *The sun is fierce; the moon has a body stained by a hare; the ocean has salty water;*  
> *Viṣṇu was a cowherd; Indra is exceedingly fickle; Śaṅkara has a blue throat;*  
> *All these have flaws; how much more so will people on earth have flaws?* || 47 ||[^2]

The commentary glosses Śaṅkara's "blue throat" as resulting from the *kālakūṭa* poison.[^3]

[^1]: Unknown, *Slokantara*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/).
[^2]: Unknown, *Slokantara*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-9647e1).
[^3]: Unknown, *Slokantara*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-c5d550).

args.query: lotus thorns snowy mountain sandalwood serpents moon hare
Viṣṇu cowherd Indra fickle Śaṅkara blue throat kālakūṭa
